projects.name: Coastal GasLink Pipeline
projects.summary: 670 km natural gas pipeline from Dawson Creek, BC to the LNG Canada export terminal at Kitimat. In service since 2023.
projects.location: Northern British Columbia (Dawson Creek to Kitimat)
projects.proponent: Coastal GasLink Pipeline Ltd. (TC Energy Corporation, with minority interests held by KKR-led consortium and Alberta Investment Management Corporation)
projects.governmentObjective: Enable LNG export from the Pacific coast as part of federal/provincial energy export strategy and reduce flaring of Western Canadian natural gas.
projects.proponentObjective: Long-term firm-service contract revenue from LNG Canada feedstock supply over the pipeline's operating life.

parties.name: 20 elected First Nation band councils along the route (impact-benefit and option agreements)

firstNationImplications.text: All 20 elected First Nation band councils whose reserve lands the route passes through signed project agreements.

sources.citing: Consent of elected councils is one form of consultation/accommodation; case law treats hereditary vs. elected authority differently.

firstNationImplications.text: Wet'suwet'en Hereditary Chiefs have publicly opposed the pipeline across their unceded traditional territory and authority.

The Delgamuukw decision recognized Wet'suwet'en oral histories of governance; the relationship between hereditary and elected authority for consent on this project remains legally and politically contested.

sources.citing: Aboriginal title affirms governance and decision-making over title lands.

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Route crosses several salmon-bearing waterways; multiple BC EAO non-compliance orders have been issued during construction relating to sediment and erosion control.
Cumulative-effects exposure: this pipeline adds to a corridor of energy infrastructure that has been the subject of treaty-rights infringement findings elsewhere in BC.

sources.citing: Yahey v. BC (2021) found cumulative effects had infringed Blueberry River First Nations' Treaty 8 rights — relevant precedent for nearby corridors.

financeRisk.text: Reported construction cost grew to approximately CAD $14.5B, more than double the original ~$6.6B estimate.
Cost overruns ultimately settled between TC Energy and LNG Canada shippers under confidential commercial arrangements; LNG Canada is the offtake counterparty.

Indigenous equity option: in 2024, TC Energy announced a 10% equity option offered to 16 First Nations along the route. Terms, financing structure, and equity carry remain partially public; community-by-community uptake decisions are ongoing.

sources.citing: CIB has been a financing pathway for similar First Nations equity transactions.

governanceQuestions: Where the elected council and hereditary chiefs differ, what process determines whose consent governs for purposes of project decision-making?
How are cumulative environmental effects with adjacent projects assessed against treaty-rights infringement standards from Yahey?
What are the disclosed terms of the 10% Indigenous equity option, and what are the consequences for First Nations equity holders if pipeline cash flow underperforms?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: What share of project cash flow, after debt service and operating costs, actually reaches our community in years 1, 5, and 10?
If the equity loan is secured against our share of cash flow, what happens if shippers renegotiate tolls or volumes?
Who decides on environmental remediation if there is a future spill, and what are our enforcement rights?
Does signing an equity agreement affect future treaty-rights litigation we may want to bring?
What independent legal and financial review has our community had, and was it funded by the proponent?

finance.structure: Privately-held by TC Energy, a KKR-led consortium, and AIMCo. Optional 10% equity tranche offered to 16 route First Nations (uptake ongoing).
finance.loanGuarantor: Not publicly disclosed for the equity tranche financing pathway as of this writing.
finance.riskCarrier: Construction-cost overruns were borne by TC Energy and resolved with shippers. Operating risk is shared among equity holders and contractually with shippers. If First Nations equity is debt-financed against cash flow, residual underperformance risk would land on the equity holder.

projects.name: Site C Clean Energy Project
projects.summary: 1,100 MW hydroelectric dam on the Peace River in northeast British Columbia. Reservoir filling underway; first power expected from initial units.
projects.location: Peace River, near Fort St. John, BC (Treaty 8 territory)
projects.governmentObjective: Add ~5,100 GWh/year of firm hydroelectric capacity to the BC grid to meet electrification, industrial, and population growth demand.
projects.proponentObjective: Deliver large-scale firm baseload generation that integrates with BC's existing hydro system.

firstNationImplications.text: West Moberly and Prophet River First Nations brought a treaty infringement claim against the project; the claim was settled in 2022 prior to a trial decision.
The settlement is confidential but BC Hydro publicly acknowledged it resolves the litigation; the precedent value of an avoided trial is therefore limited.
Reservoir flooding affects burial sites, fishing sites, hunting territories, and cultural sites; the long-term cultural-loss impact extends across generations.

sources.citing: Right to pursue usual vocations of hunting, trapping and fishing under Treaty 8.

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Following the 2021 Yahey decision in favour of Blueberry River First Nations on cumulative effects in the same region, BC's broader approach to authorizing development in Treaty 8 territory has been under sustained legal and political pressure.
Reservoir conversion of riverine ecosystem to lake-like conditions affects sediment transport, fish habitat, and downstream flow regime for decades.

financeRisk.text: Capital cost grew from a 2014 estimate of CAD $7.9B to approximately CAD $16B (BC Hydro's 2021 reforecast).
Cost is ultimately borne by BC ratepayers through electricity rates; the Province of BC has provided rate relief mechanisms.
If long-run electricity demand falls short of projections (e.g. industrial load loss), the cost-recovery burden per ratepayer rises.

governanceQuestions: What public-interest test would a similarly situated project face today under DRIPA and the post-Yahey consultation framework?
What governance role do affected First Nations have over reservoir-related decisions (e.g. ramping, debris management, future cultural-site protection) for the operating life of the dam?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: What enforceable commitments exist around access to traditional territory now under reservoir?
What is the long-term flow regime, and what monitoring will affected communities have access to?
What is the precedent value of the West Moberly / Prophet River settlement for future projects in our territory?

finance.structure: Crown corporation balance sheet; ultimately backstopped by Province of BC.
finance.loanGuarantor: Province of British Columbia (Crown corporation debt)
finance.riskCarrier: BC ratepayers via electricity rates; with Provincial backstop if Hydro's financial position required intervention.

projects.name: Trans Mountain Expansion Project (TMX)
projects.summary: Twinned crude-oil pipeline from Edmonton, AB to Burnaby, BC, nearly tripling nameplate capacity from ~300,000 to ~890,000 bbl/day. In service since May 2024.
projects.location: Edmonton, AB to Burnaby, BC (with Westridge Marine Terminal expansion)
projects.proponent: Trans Mountain Corporation (federal Crown corporation since 2018)
projects.governmentObjective: Provide tidewater access for Alberta heavy crude to reach Asia-Pacific markets and reduce price discounts on Canadian crude.
projects.proponentObjective: Operate the pipeline to recover capital cost via committed shipper tolls under regulated tariffs.

parties.name: 129 Indigenous groups along the route (with mutual benefit agreements, per Trans Mountain Corp.)
Government of Canada (sole shareholder via Canada TMP Finance Ltd.)

firstNationImplications.text: The Federal Court of Appeal in 2018 (Tsleil-Waututh Nation v. Canada) found that the second-round Phase III consultation was inadequate and quashed the original federal approval; project was re-approved in 2019 after a remedial consultation process.

sources.citing: Duty-to-consult standard applied in TMX litigation.

firstNationImplications.text: Multiple First Nation-led equity acquisition proposals (e.g. Project Reconciliation, Nesika, Iron Coalition) have been publicly discussed; no transaction has been announced as of this writing.

If a sale to an Indigenous-led consortium were structured with CIB or comparable government support, the financing structure and risk-allocation terms would materially affect the outcome for participating Nations.

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: Tanker traffic in Burrard Inlet associated with the expanded Westridge Marine Terminal raises documented marine-spill and Southern Resident Killer Whale concerns that were central to the 2018 FCA decision.
Coldwater Indian Band raised specific concerns about a routing alignment over their sole-source aquifer; the federal regulator and Trans Mountain have engaged on routing alternatives.

financeRisk.text: Capital cost grew from a 2017 estimate of ~CAD $7.4B to approximately CAD $34B at completion (Trans Mountain Corporation disclosure).
Government of Canada has signalled intent to divest its interest in the project; the eventual sale price, buyer composition, and Indigenous participation terms remain open.
If tolls or shipper-commitment volumes prove insufficient to recover the full capital cost, the federal Crown carries the residual exposure until a divestment closes.

governanceQuestions: What is the federal government's framework for evaluating Indigenous-led acquisition proposals, and how is fair price determined when the seller is the Crown and the buyer is partly Crown-financed?
How will environmental obligations (decommissioning, spill response, marine impacts) be preserved across a change of ownership?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: If our Nation is offered a stake in a future Indigenous-led acquisition, what is the cash-flow waterfall — who gets paid first, and where do we sit?
What is the indemnity structure for legacy environmental liabilities — do they stay with the Crown, transfer to buyers, or get split?
What veto, observer, or governance rights does an equity stake convey, distinct from cash returns?

finance.structure: Federal Crown corporation. Acquired by Canada in 2018 for CAD $4.5B. Construction completed in 2024 at ≈ CAD $34B.
finance.loanGuarantor: Government of Canada (via Canada TMP Finance Ltd.)
finance.riskCarrier: Government of Canada (federal taxpayers) until a divestment closes; tolls and shipper commitments are the recovery mechanism.

primarySources.citing: Potential financing pathway for an Indigenous-led acquisition

projects.name: Cedar LNG
projects.summary: ≈ 3.3 Mtpa floating LNG export facility at Kitimat, BC. Indigenous-majority ownership: Haisla Nation 50.1%, Pembina Pipeline Corporation 49.9%.
projects.proponent: Cedar LNG Partners LP (Haisla Nation 50.1%, Pembina 49.9%)
projects.governmentObjective: Demonstrate Indigenous-led major-project ownership at scale; expand Pacific LNG export capacity.
projects.proponentObjective: Generate long-term LNG export revenue under offtake contracts; for Haisla, build multi-decade community wealth and governance capacity.

parties.name: Export Development Canada (project finance role disclosed)

firstNationImplications.text: Haisla Nation is the majority equity owner, not merely an impact-benefit beneficiary. This represents a structurally different model from most legacy LNG projects.
Project crosses multiple First Nations' interests via shipping channels; engagement with Haisla, Gitga'at, and others has been documented in the EA.
Long-run effects on Haisla governance capacity, intergenerational wealth, and exposure to global LNG price volatility will be material over the project's operating life.

treatyAndWaterRisk.text: As an electrically-driven liquefaction facility, lifecycle emissions intensity is reported as lower than gas-driven LNG comparators.
Marine impacts (LNG carrier traffic in Douglas Channel, sensitive marine habitat) are part of the EA conditions.

financeRisk.text: Final investment decision was taken in 2024 with a capital cost estimate of approximately CAD $4B.
Haisla's 50.1% equity will be financed in part through external debt; specific lenders and loan-guarantee structure include disclosed federal participation.

sources.citing: CIB has been publicly identified in this transaction; verify current terms in CIB and project disclosures.

financeRisk.text: LNG price exposure: Cedar's revenue depends on global LNG prices through its offtake agreements. Sustained low-price environment would compress cash flows available to debt service and to Haisla's equity distribution.

governanceQuestions: How are project-level decisions (capex deferrals, operating decisions, future expansions) governed between the 50.1% Indigenous equity holder and the operating partner?
What sequencing of debt repayment vs. community distribution applies, and how is it disclosed to Haisla members?
recommendedCommunityQuestions: For Haisla members: what is our annual expected distribution after debt service, in low / base / high LNG price scenarios?
What independent governance and audit structures exist to ensure the partnership delivers as designed?
What is the long-tail decommissioning plan, and who carries the obligation?

finance.structure: Cedar LNG Partners LP. Haisla Nation 50.1%, Pembina 49.9%. Indigenous equity financed in part through debt with disclosed federal participation.
finance.loanGuarantor: Federal participation has been publicly disclosed; verify specifics in CIB and project filings.
finance.riskCarrier: Project equity holders bear operating and price risk; lenders bear default risk subject to any government participation; Haisla cash flow waterfall sits after debt service.

primarySources.citing: Federal financing pathway for Indigenous-majority equity
Implementation context for Indigenous-led project ownership
